summary: Gruber analyzes Microsoft's Surface announcement as a watershed moment driven not by design ambition but by economic necessity. He argues the event was poorly rehearsed and lacked coherent messaging, with Microsoft unable to make hard decisions between ARM and Intel, soft and hard keyboards. The real significance, he contends, is that Microsoft's traditional software licensing model is no longer sustainable in a post-iPad world where Apple earns $195 in profit per iPad versus Microsoft's $78 per PC. Surface is less about competing with Apple on design than about Microsoft needing to capture hardware margins to survive. Gruber argues the move primarily threatens Microsoft's own OEM partners rather than Apple, since the OEMs have nowhere else to go. He concludes that Microsoft will inevitably acquire Nokia.
key_points: The Surface event was rushed and under-rehearsed, revealing Microsoft's inability to make decisive design choices — shipping two incompatible tablets with two different keyboard covers
Microsoft's software licensing model yields only $78 profit per PC versus Apple's $195 profit per iPad, making the old hardware/software split unsustainable
Surface is fundamentally about Microsoft needing to sell integrated hardware+software to maintain profitability, not about competing with iPad on design quality
The move primarily threatens Microsoft's OEM partners (Dell, HP, Toshiba) rather than Apple, since those OEMs have no viable alternative platform
Apple's competitors in phones are already starving for profit, and the PC market is heading toward the same consolidation
money_quotes: Design is about making decisions, and Microsoft could not decide.
The message I took away is that Microsoft has concluded that, ready or not, it needed to move now. There is no longer enough profit to be had selling software alone.
The iPhone suggested Apple would dominate pocket computing. The iPad suggests Apple will dominate computing, period.
Apple doesn't want to sell all of the world's computers and phones; rather, Apple wants to reap all the profits in the computer and phone industries.
Their business model of expensive software on cheap hardware is not sustainable. The future is nearly free software integrated into moderately priced hardware.
Nothing works sustainably in business without profit — profit is the oxygen companies breathe.
It's the OEMs whom Microsoft thinks Surface can put into checkmate, not Apple.
If I'm right, it's inevitable now that Microsoft will acquire Nokia.
key_insight: Microsoft Surface was not a design play to match the iPad but an economic necessity — the old model of selling expensive software on cheap OEM hardware could no longer sustain Microsoft's profitability in a world where Apple proved integrated hardware-software commands all the margin.
